title: Good Heavens! I Am Ashamed Even to Tell It!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below preserves the current OCR transcription as a reading copy; the scanned source remains authoritative. Broken list markers, page-boundary joins, and isolated OCR fragments are resolved in English only where the printed sense is unambiguous. The rapid rhetorical questions, sarcasm, repeated exclamations, and closing reference to a “Tughlaq durbar” are intentionally retained as features of Kalaignar's voice. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: What is happening in Tamil Nadu? Is there anything that can be called a government for the people? Do those called ministers come to the Secretariat and attend to administration? Are files moving? Does anyone possess even an atom of concern or care about any of this? Open the newspapers and every minister shows his face. But what public work are they doing? Were they not elected by the people to serve the people? Yet every one of the ministers is camped in R. K. Nagar! Whether they are actually there or not, their faces appear in the papers.

If a newspaper must carry a story saying that a minister met the public in the constituency and sought votes, there is one charge. If the story must appear with a photograph, there is another charge. The “agents” receive a separate commission! Somehow, news of their campaigning must reach the sacred attention of Her Divine Lady Amma.

They investigate which newspaper “Amma” reads and burn with impatience to have that paper report that they are campaigning without pause! Is there some fierce contest in R. K. Nagar? All the major parties have withdrawn, saying they want none of this trouble. In a constituency without an opponent, all these ministers are supposedly campaigning and asking for votes with swaggering boasts! Like swinging a sword through empty air, it is a hollow, ostentatious campaign! For this, all the ministers have apparently formed teams and are making distributions there.

What other news has appeared in the papers besides their campaigning? In this evening's papers alone, dated 15 June 2015: sixty sovereigns stolen in successive burglaries at four houses in Tambaram and Adambakkam; seventeen sovereigns of jewellery stolen from an Amman temple at Karapakkam; one hundred sovereigns stolen from three successive houses in Chengalpattu; a chain snatched from a Marwari woman, who wandered about with an infant in her arms trying to lodge a complaint; a red-sanders agent kidnapped near Tiruvannamalai for a ransom of Rs. 50 lakh; thirty-nine sovereigns and cash stolen after a house lock was broken in Adambakkam; Rs. 5 lakh in cash and jewellery stolen in a house burglary; eleven sovereigns stolen from a sub-inspector's house in Tambaram; and burglaries at three houses and a temple in South Chennai, all on the same day.

These are merely the reports from one evening! What is surprising about that? One may ask whether such reports do not appear every day. Yes, they continue to appear every day. But the rulers have neither the time nor the inclination to notice or think about them. Why not? What are they doing? They are campaigning day and night without pause in R. K. Nagar. Against whom are they campaigning? Against no one. Then why campaign? To campaign in support of Amma! But when there is no opposition, why campaign? What can they do? Must their ministerial posts not survive?

“For the past seven or eight months we went from temple to temple. We performed rituals and restoration ceremonies. We shaved our heads, conducted sacrificial rites, carried milk *kavadi*, and dressed ourselves in neem leaves. Every vow was for Amma's release. No, no; it was to preserve our ministerial posts! Now that Amma has been released, we have come to campaign for her,” they would say.

But what is happening in the State? Contractors openly told reporters that officials in Tamil Nadu's Public Works Department were demanding commissions of up to forty-five per cent. They erected flex boards. As a first step, they published the names of ten corrupt officials. On 9 May, they even submitted a complaint to the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption. What happened? Was any inquiry conducted?

Instead, the contractors who submitted the complaint were tracked down and pressed into compromise talks. The Contractors' Association itself was split in two. Action was taken within the Association against those who complained. The Public Works Department leadership also issued an oral order that they should receive no further contract work. Is that all? A decision was made to scrutinise every job those contractors had previously completed and discover some defect or offence in it. The contractors have said that they will not fear such acts of revenge. What rules Tamil Nadu is a reckless government that punishes anyone who points out and questions wrongdoing in the administration.

People of Tamil Nadu! Those with a conscience in every political party! I ask you to think for a few minutes. What indeed is happening in Tamil Nadu? Is anything resembling democratic government taking place?

What happened in Tamil Nadu after Jayalalithaa was convicted in the disproportionate-assets case by the Karnataka Special Court on 27 September last year? There was one person called the Chief Minister and another called the “People's Chief Minister”! A Chief Minister who would not even sit in the room meant for the Chief Minister; a person who acted as though he felt shy even to call himself Chief Minister. Photographs of the Chief Minister in government offices and buildings were not changed. Six months of this year have passed, and even the diary meant for Members of the Legislative Assembly has not yet been printed and distributed! Is there something called an Assembly Secretariat functioning? Is there someone called an Assembly Secretary? Has he not continued in office for years on extension, waving the ceremonial fan for those in power? How could he publish a diary? Even the government calendar that appeared did not contain the Chief Minister's photograph.

Panneerselvam held office as Chief Minister for five or six months. Did a single government function take place during those months? Did he attend one? Did he inaugurate any building or bridge? Then why was he Chief Minister? Is this a country at all?

And what great work is happening now? Every day, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa arrives at the Secretariat at about one in the afternoon, poses for photographs supposedly inaugurating one building or another by video conference and consulting a minister, supplies the photographs and reports to the press, and returns home at about three.

What has she done in the twenty days since assuming office? On 25 May 2015, her first day in office, reports said that with the Chief Secretary and Adviser standing beside her, she approved and signed five schemes worth Rs. 3,047 crore.

The Governor's Address in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly was completed, the Budget was read, and the general debate ended. Yet to this day there has been no debate on the demands for grants. Because those demands have not been passed, work in many departments remains paralysed. The Rs. 3,047 crore in schemes announced by the Chief Minister did not appear in the Budget. Only after the Budget debate had ended and Jayalalithaa returned as Chief Minister did this hurried announcement arrive.

Do you know what happened on the following days? On 27 May, she reportedly inaugurated police stations, new quarters and other buildings worth Rs. 444 crore; on 30 May, substations worth Rs. 2,154.26 crore; on 31 May, combined drinking-water schemes worth Rs. 814 crore; on 3 June, new Highways Department bridges worth Rs. 389 crore; on 4 June, District Collectorate buildings worth Rs. 111.24 crore; on 9 June, irrigation-improvement schemes worth Rs. 100 crore; on 10 June, School Education Department buildings worth Rs. 281 crore; on 11 June, new municipal and town-panchayat offices, sanitation complexes, parks and drainage schemes worth Rs. 123.08 crore for the Municipal Administration and Water Supply Department; and on 15 June, eighty student hostels built at a cost of Rs. 62 crore. Every day the newspapers report that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa inaugurated them by video conference.

Add the projects inaugurated by Jayalalithaa thus far, and their value is Rs. 4,898.47 crore. Were all these buildings suddenly constructed and completed only after she assumed office? If they had already been completed, why were they not opened while Panneerselvam was Chief Minister? Why were buildings constructed with the tax money poured in by the people left unused all these days? How long had they stood completed but unopened? Who was responsible?

And there is another curiosity! Newspapers reported that Jayalalithaa inaugurated the Government Engineering College at Bodinayakanur. But that college was apparently opened six months ago and has been functioning ever since! These are fitting examples of the absurd spectacles staged under AIADMK rule. In previous years, Jayalalithaa prevented every minister from making even a single announcement and advertised herself as making every department's announcements under Rule 110. Does the country now need another drama in the same style, in which buildings already opened are supposedly inaugurated again?

I ask: has any other administration witnessed wrongdoing and irregularity on the scale seen during these four years? Do the people not know how many times Jayalalithaa reshuffled ministers because their misconduct left her unable to answer? Why were some ministers against whom action had been taken later restored to office? Did she imagine that the people had forgotten their offences? Or were the irregularities covered up? If those ministers were not removed for wrongdoing, why were they removed? Do the people who voted have no right to know?

A minister was removed and an AIADMK functionary arrested over corruption in Aavin milk. What became of that case? A senior government officer was pressured to appoint certain people. Because of that pressure, he left his family in the street and took his own life by falling before a train. A minister was proceeded against and arrested. What became of that case?

Is action against one minister alone sufficient for Muthukumarasamy's death? Does a Cabinet not bear collective responsibility? Should this entire Cabinet not answer for his suicide? What is happening now? Are efforts being made to dilute the case, or are they not?

What became of the red-sanders smuggling case? Who were the political figures involved? Are efforts being made to divert that case too, or not? Concealing all such wrongdoing, what is the haste to win a by-election? Has there ever before been a precedent in which a constituency was declared vacant and a by-election announced within a few days?

What allegations arose against the brother of Panneerselvam, who served as Jayalalithaa's proxy Chief Minister and today stands next to her in rank? A priest from a Scheduled Caste community who died by suicide left a handwritten note naming the minister's brother Raja as the cause of his death. What became of that case? Last year, Jayalalithaa herself presented that Raja with an award for Best Municipal Chairman. Reports then said that his chairmanship would be taken away and that he would be arrested. What happened? Was everything buried?

Only yesterday, in another case alleging that an AIADMK municipal chairperson committed land fraud using forged documents, the High Court itself ordered action against her. Are these not shameful events that have befallen these rulers? How many complaints, acts of corruption, irregularities and disastrous deeds hostile to democracy and the people can one recount? Good heavens! I am ashamed even to tell it!

Although Justice Kumaraswamy's acquittal in the appeal has temporarily allowed Jayalalithaa to be called innocent, I ask what a Supreme Court judge asked in the TANSI case: is Jayalalithaa innocent according to her conscience? Who is responsible for the addition error in Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment? Is that total correct? Has Jayalalithaa or anyone in her party offered an explanation to this day?

Yet without the slightest concern about any of this, there was a hurried swearing-in, a hurried by-election announcement, and a hurried contest. Is this arrogance born of the belief that the people of Tamil Nadu can be deceived for as long as they please? Do the publications that call themselves impartial newspapers in Tamil Nadu open their mouths about any of this to honour democracy and protect public welfare? Certainly not. They take delight in their vow of silence! Long live the ethics of the Tamil Nadu press! May the Tughlaq durbar flourish!
With affection,
M. K. 17 June 2015
